steps.title: Find base angles of isosceles triangle ACD
steps.content:
In triangle ACD, we have $AC = CD$ (isosceles) and $\angle ACD = 108°$. The base angles are equal:
$$\angle CAD = \angle CDA = \frac{180° - 108°}{2} = \frac{72°}{2} = 36°$$

steps.title: Set up angle relationships using collinearity
steps.content:
Points D, C, and B lie on the same line. Since D–C–B are collinear, angles $\angle DCE$ and $\angle BCE$ are supplementary:
$$\angle BCE = 180° - \angle DCE$$
In triangle DCE, since $\angle CDE = 36°$:
$$\angle DCE + \angle DEC = 180° - 36° = 144°$$
Substituting:
$$\angle BCE = 180° - (144° - \angle DEC) = 36° + \angle DEC$$

steps.title: Show angle BED is constant using angle EBC = 42°
steps.content:
In triangle BCE, the angles sum to \$180°$: $$\angle EBC + \angle BCE + \angle BEC = 180°$$ $$42° + (36° + \angle DEC) + \angle BEC = 180°$$ $$\angle BEC = 102° - \angle DEC$$ The angle BED combines these adjacent angles: $$\angle BED = \angle BEC + \angle DEC = (102° - \angle DEC) + \angle DEC = 102°$$
Key insight: The variable cancels, making $\angle BED = 102°$ regardless of where E lies on line DA.

steps.title: Find x using collinearity of D, E, A
steps.content: Since D, E, and A are collinear, angles BED and BEA are supplementary: $$x = \angle BEA = 180° - \angle BED = 180° - 102° = 78°$$ That matches choice 78.
